feedback.text: Hello, the Digital Fairness Act seems to me to be a promising initiative in terms of consumer protection, unfair commercial practices or exploiting user vulnerabilities. I think it would be useful to consider also the current commercial practices of selling computer software, in particular video games. Indeed, it has become common for the sale of a video game to be accompanied by a contract of use stipulating inter alia that the publisher may at any time unilaterally prevent users from enjoying the use of the product they have purchased. Although this may be understood, since publishers cannot offer life-saving support to all users, whether voluntarily or not, it is however not normal that a product that has been sold to a consumer can be removed from it without warning. This may be all the more serious in the case of video games, since they are cultural and artistic products, and the end of the medium by their publisher means not only the inability of buyers to enjoy them, but also, too often, the outright death of the work. It therefore seems important to me to consider that aspect of the marketing of software, for example by requiring publishers to make available to users the tools necessary to be able to benefit from their products, even after the end of their life. Or by banning certain digital rights management (DRM) technologies, or even some anti-tricking systems in video games, which are often too invasive, for example by requiring users to have permanent internet access to the publisher’s servers, even when the use of the software does not require it. Or to prohibit the publisher from imposing the use of its servers for the online functionalities of their software, preventing users from deciding on the location of their data, and requiring the publisher to trust the server management, such as the Minecraft game. There have also been several recent cases showing, if not an abuse by publishers, at least a neglect of consumers’ rights to several video products, such as in the case of The Crew video game, where the publisher removed the game altogether from the users’ library, without even preventing them, and thus prevented them from playing offline despite the closure of the servers. Or the Concord video game, whose end of life was declared only two weeks after it was launched, and whose development studio saw the same fate two months later. In addition, there are more and more ‘video games as a service’ that are exiting, and this game model seems to be the default model of ‘AAA’ games. However, it is a model conducive to unfair commercial practices such as those covered by the Digital Service Act. As it is often fully or partially online, it is particularly concerned by these unannounced support purposes, since the closure of servers often means that it is impossible to play on them. And if this model were to be even more widespread, it could mean a cultural hecatomb in the video landscape. It is therefore for me a model that needs to be regulated because it is recent, common and gives publishers a strong power over users. All of these situations seem to me to be standard practices in the market for computer software and seem to me to be very dangerous for consumer rights, just like the issues currently mentioned in the Digital Service Act. And that is why I would like to ask you to add it. Thank you for your attention to this comment. Warmly,
